era_context: Trajan's reign (98–117 CE) is in its final years, with the Parthian Campaign still in progress. The Kitos War continues across the eastern Mediterranean. The Eastern Han Dynasty in China remains stable under Emperor An. The Kushan Empire controls the Silk Road. This is a period of sustained military conflict and imperial consolidation.
geographic_coverage_gaps: India (limited contemporary sources for this specific year)

events.title: Trajan's Parthian Campaign in advanced stages
events.description: Trajan's forces continue their advance through Mesopotamia, moving closer to Ctesiphon. The campaign is in its advanced stages, with Roman legions consolidating control over conquered territories. The logistical challenges of maintaining such an extended supply line are significant, but Roman military organization proves capable of sustaining the effort. The campaign represents the apex of Roman imperial military power.
events.certainty_note: The general progression of the campaign is well-documented, but specific events within 111 CE are not precisely dated in surviving sources. The year is assigned based on the overall timeline of the campaign (114–116 CE).

events.title: Kitos War in final stages
events.description: The Jewish uprising continues, though Roman suppression efforts are intensifying. Quietus and other Roman generals are actively engaged in suppressing the revolt across multiple regions. The rebellion is gradually being crushed, though pockets of resistance persist. The conflict has resulted in massive destruction of Jewish communities and significant loss of life.
key_figures: Quietus (Roman general, sent to suppress the revolt)
events.certainty_note: The continuation of the Kitos War into 111 CE is inferred from the overall timeline (115–117 CE). Specific events within this year are not well-documented in surviving written sources.

events.title: Eastern Han Dynasty maintains control
events.description: The Eastern Han Dynasty continues under Emperor An, maintaining administrative stability and control over the Silk Road trade routes. The empire manages relationships with nomadic groups on the northern frontier and maintains internal order. This period represents the height of Eastern Han prosperity before the dynasty's decline in the late 2nd century CE.

sources.name: Historiographic consensus on Eastern Han chronology

events.certainty_note: Eastern Han chronology is well-established through Chinese historical records. Emperor An's reign dates are secure.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact timeline of the Parthian Campaign's final stages is debated. Some sources suggest Ctesiphon was captured in 115 CE, while others place it in 116 CE. The sustainability of Roman control over Mesopotamia is questioned by modern historians—the territory was abandoned within a few years, suggesting the conquest was not strategically viable. The Kitos War's exact end date is also debated, with some sources suggesting it ended in 116 CE and others placing the final suppression in 117 CE.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 111 CE is heavily dependent on Cassius Dio and other late sources. No contemporary accounts survive from either the Roman or Jewish perspective. Archaeological evidence from affected regions provides material confirmation of destruction but not precise dating to individual years. The narrative is shaped by Roman imperial historiography, which emphasizes Trajan's military achievements. The Parthian Campaign receives more attention in surviving sources than the Kitos War.

graph_edges.note: Campaign continuation is part of the Parthian Campaign
Campaign advances lead to capture of Ctesiphon in 116 CE
Continuation of the Kitos War that began in 115 CE
Suppression efforts lead to final suppression in 117 CE
